statusLine: Americas oil markets stabilize with WTI at $80.59/bbl after 5% weekly decline, as US crude inventories draw down significantly amid heightened Middle East shipping risks.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets showed signs of stabilization following WTI's 5.05% decline in the week ending June 15, settling at $80.59/bbl. The weekly pullback brought crude back from elevated levels, though prices remain supported by strong domestic fundamentals. US commercial crude inventories fell sharply by 7.2 million barrels to 426.5 million barrels, indicating robust demand despite seasonal maintenance patterns. US production held steady at 13.8 million barrels per day, maintaining America's position as the world's top producer.

The $2.65 WTI discount to Brent crude reflects persistent logistical advantages for Americas-sourced oil, even as global shipping routes face severe disruption. Critical maritime advisories remain active across multiple Middle Eastern chokepoints, with Houthi attacks in the Red Sea and Iranian threats in the Strait of Hormuz forcing tankers into longer, costlier routes around Africa. This shipping crisis continues to support the relative value proposition of Western Hemisphere crude supplies.

US retail fuel prices declined alongside crude, with gasoline falling 15.8 cents to $4.28/gallon and diesel down 14 cents to $5.21/gallon, providing some relief to consumers ahead of peak summer driving season. The modest gasoline inventory build of 186,000 barrels suggests refiners are adequately positioned for seasonal demand, while distillate stocks declined by 200,000 barrels.

Across the broader Americas, producers from Canada's oil sands to Brazil's pre-salt fields are benefiting from the Middle East supply route disruptions, though specific production data for June remains limited. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve held at 349.2 million barrels, well below historical averages but providing some emergency supply cushion amid ongoing geopolitical tensions.
keyPoints: WTI crude fell 5.05% week-on-week to $80.59/bbl in the week ending June 15, stabilizing after recent volatility
US commercial crude inventories dropped sharply by 7.2 million barrels, signaling strong domestic demand
Critical shipping disruptions persist in Red Sea and Persian Gulf, supporting Americas crude premiums
US retail gasoline prices fell 15.8 cents to $4.28/gal, providing consumer relief ahead of summer driving season
US crude production steady at 13.8 million bpd, maintaining global leadership position
WTI trades at $2.65 discount to Brent, reflecting logistical advantages of Western Hemisphere supplies
